cards.back: It means the value of the variable x that makes the function as small as possible over the domain being considered.
It means the value of the variable x that makes the function as large as possible over the domain being considered.

cards.front: What equation identifies an interior stationary point of f(x)?
cards.back: The first-order condition is f'(x*)=0. This is why optimization can be treated as a root-finding problem on the derivative.

cards.front: What does f''(x*)>0 suggest at a stationary point?
cards.back: It suggests a local minimum because the curve bends upward near the point.

cards.front: What does f''(x*)<0 suggest at a stationary point?
cards.back: It suggests a local maximum because the curve bends downward near the point.

cards.front: Why is optimization called an extension of equation solving in this lesson?
cards.back: Because the condition for an interior optimum is f'(x)=0, so locating stationary points becomes a root-finding task.

cards.front: Why must endpoints be checked on a closed interval optimization problem?
cards.back: Because the global minimum or maximum may occur at a boundary point even if interior stationary points also exist.

cards.front: Near x*, what term mainly controls f'(x_k) in Taylor expansion?
cards.back: The dominant local term is usually (x_k-x*)f''(x*), so curvature controls how the derivative behaves near the optimum.

cards.front: If you are minimizing and f'(x_k)>0, which direction should you move?
cards.back: You should move left, meaning decrease x, because positive slope means the function is increasing as x increases.

cards.front: What is the difference between a stationary point and a confirmed optimum?
cards.back: A stationary point only satisfies f'(x)=0. A confirmed optimum also needs classification, such as a second-derivative test or endpoint comparison.
